beginner.question: What does CSS stand for and what is its primary purpose?
beginner.answer: CSS stands for Cascading Style Sheets. Its primary purpose is to control the visual presentation and layout of HTML content — including colors, fonts, spacing, positioning, and responsive behavior — separating content (HTML) from presentation (CSS).

beginner.question: What are the three ways to apply CSS to an HTML document?
beginner.answer: The three methods are: (1) External CSS — a separate .css file linked via <link> in the <head>, which is the best practice; (2) Internal CSS — CSS placed inside a <style> tag in the <head>, useful for single pages; (3) Inline CSS — CSS applied directly via the style attribute on an element, least preferred due to maintenance difficulties.

beginner.question: What is the basic structure of a CSS rule?
beginner.answer: A CSS rule consists of a selector that targets HTML elements, followed by a declaration block enclosed in curly braces {}. Each declaration is a property-value pair separated by a colon, with each declaration ending in a semicolon. Example: h1 { color: blue; font-size: 24px; }

beginner.question: What does the 'cascading' part of CSS mean?
beginner.answer: The cascade is the algorithm browsers use to resolve conflicting style rules. When multiple rules apply to the same element, precedence is determined by: (1) Importance (!important overrides everything); (2) Specificity (more specific selectors win); (3) Source order (later rules override earlier ones when specificity is equal).

beginner.question: Why is external CSS recommended over inline or internal CSS?
beginner.answer: External CSS separates content from presentation, allows a single stylesheet to control multiple pages, is cacheable by browsers (improving load times on subsequent pages), and is easier to maintain and update. Inline CSS mixes content with presentation and is hard to maintain, while internal CSS is not reusable across pages.

beginner.question: What is the difference between HTML and CSS responsibilities?
beginner.answer: HTML is responsible for structure and content (headings, paragraphs, images, links) — the 'what.' CSS is responsible for presentation and layout (colors, fonts, spacing, positioning) — the 'how.' A common analogy: HTML is the skeleton/blueprint while CSS is the paint, furniture, and interior design.

beginner.question: How do you link an external CSS file to an HTML document?
beginner.answer: You use the <link> element inside the HTML <head>, with rel="stylesheet" to specify the relationship and href to specify the path to the CSS file. Example: <link rel="stylesheet" href="styles.css">

beginner.question: What is inheritance in CSS?
beginner.answer: Inheritance means some CSS properties are automatically passed from parent elements to their children. For example, setting color or font-family on a <body> element will be inherited by all child elements. However, not all properties inherit — box-model properties like margin, padding, and border are not inherited by default.

beginner.question: How can you inspect and debug CSS in a browser?
beginner.answer: You can open browser Developer Tools (DevTools) by pressing F12 or right-clicking and selecting 'Inspect.' DevTools allows you to view applied CSS rules, temporarily edit styles in real-time, inspect the box model, debug layout issues, and test responsive designs.

beginner.question: What is the separation of concerns principle in web development?
beginner.answer: Separation of concerns means keeping different responsibilities in distinct layers: HTML for structure/semantics, CSS for presentation/styling, and JavaScript for behavior/interactivity. This makes code more maintainable, reusable, and accessible, and allows specialists to work on each layer independently.

intermediate.question: What are user-agent stylesheets and how do they affect CSS?
intermediate.answer: User-agent stylesheets are default styles applied by browsers to HTML elements. Each browser has its own defaults (e.g., margins on <body>, font sizes on headings). These defaults can vary between browsers, which is why CSS resets or normalize.css are used to create a consistent baseline before applying custom styles.

intermediate.question: When would you choose internal CSS over external CSS?
intermediate.answer: Internal CSS is appropriate for single-page websites, quick prototypes, email templates, or when you need to override specific styles for a single page without creating a separate file. It can also be useful for content management systems where you don't have access to the external stylesheet. However, for multi-page sites, external CSS is always preferred.

intermediate.question: Explain the cascade priority pyramid from lowest to highest specificity.
intermediate.answer: From lowest to highest specificity: (1) Universal selector (*) — 0-0-0-0; (2) Element/pseudo-element selectors — 0-0-0-1; (3) Class, attribute, and pseudo-class selectors — 0-0-1-0; (4) ID selectors — 0-1-0-0; (5) Inline styles — 1-0-0-0; (6) !important annotations — override everything regardless of specificity.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between the @import method and the <link> method for loading CSS?
intermediate.answer: The <link> tag in HTML is the recommended method because it allows parallel downloading of CSS files and doesn't block rendering as much. @import is a CSS directive that must be at the top of a stylesheet and blocks parallel downloads — the browser must wait for the importing stylesheet to load before downloading the imported one, causing slower page load times.

intermediate.question: What is a Flash of Unstyled Content (FOUC) and how do you prevent it?
intermediate.answer: FOUC occurs when a web page loads the HTML content briefly without CSS applied, causing a brief flash of unstyled content before the stylesheet loads. To prevent it, always place <link> tags in the HTML <head> section so styles load before the page renders, and avoid using @import which delays CSS loading.

intermediate.question: How does browser developer tools help you understand the cascade?
intermediate.answer: DevTools shows the 'Computed' styles panel which lists all CSS properties applied to an element, along with the source file and line number for each rule. It also shows which rules are overridden (struck through), making it easy to see the cascade in action and debug specificity conflicts.

intermediate.question: What properties are typically inherited and which are not? Give examples.
intermediate.answer: Inherited properties include color, font-family, font-size, font-weight, line-height, text-align, and visibility. Non-inherited properties include margin, padding, border, background, width, height, position, display, overflow, and z-index. The inherit keyword can force inheritance for any property.

intermediate.question: What does the CSS validator do and why should you use it?
intermediate.answer: The W3C CSS Validator checks CSS code against the official CSS specifications and reports syntax errors, unsupported properties, incorrect values, and other issues. Using it ensures your CSS is standards-compliant, helps catch errors before deployment, and improves cross-browser compatibility.

intermediate.question: How does minification help CSS performance?
intermediate.answer: CSS minification removes unnecessary characters like whitespace, comments, newlines, and extra semicolons without changing functionality. This reduces file size significantly (often 30-50% smaller), which means faster download times, less bandwidth usage, and improved page load speed, especially on slow connections.

intermediate.question: What is graceful degradation in CSS and why is it important?
intermediate.answer: Graceful degradation means designing CSS so that modern browsers get the full visual experience while older browsers still receive a functional, usable version. For example, using CSS Grid with a flexbox fallback ensures that older browsers that don't support Grid still display a usable layout. This approach maximizes compatibility without sacrificing modern features.

advanced.question: Explain the exact algorithm the browser uses to resolve the cascade, in order.
advanced.answer: The cascade resolution algorithm follows these steps in order: (1) Relevance — filter rules targeting the element; (2) Origin and importance — sort by origin (!important overrides normal, author overrides user overrides user-agent); (3) Specificity — compute a 4-part specificity value (inline, IDs, classes/attributes/pseudo-classes, elements/pseudo-elements); (4) Order — later declarations override earlier ones when specificity is equal; (5) Initial/inherit/unset/revert — handle these special keyword values.

advanced.question: What are the six origins of CSS and what is their priority order?
advanced.answer: The six origins in priority order (lowest to highest for normal declarations): user-agent styles (browser defaults), user normal styles (user browser settings), author normal styles (web developer CSS), author !important, user !important, user-agent !important. This means user !important has the highest authority, allowing users with accessibility needs to override website styles.

advanced.question: How does the CSSOM (CSS Object Model) relate to the DOM and how does it affect rendering?
advanced.answer: The CSSOM is a tree-like structure the browser builds from CSS rules, parallel to the DOM tree built from HTML. Both trees are combined into a render tree that includes only visible elements with their computed styles. The CSSOM construction blocks rendering — the browser cannot display content until both DOM and CSSOM are built. This is why CSS is considered a render-blocking resource.

advanced.question: Explain how the 'unset', 'initial', 'revert', and 'revert-layer' keywords work in the cascade.
advanced.answer: 'initial' sets a property to its CSS specification default value. 'inherit' forces inheritance even for non-inherited properties. 'unset' acts as 'inherit' for inherited properties and 'initial' for non-inherited ones. 'revert' resets to the value that would have been applied from the earlier cascade origins (user-agent, user, or author layers). 'revert-layer' reverts to the previous cascade layer's value.

advanced.question: What is the render-blocking behavior of CSS and how do you optimize critical CSS?
advanced.answer: CSS is render-blocking because the browser must build the CSSOM before rendering any content. To optimize, identify critical above-the-fold CSS and inline it directly in the HTML <head>, while loading non-critical CSS asynchronously using media="print" onload techniques or the 'loadCSS' pattern. This allows the browser to render the visible content immediately while deferred CSS loads in the background.

advanced.question: How do browser-specific vendor prefixes interact with the cascade, and what is the Autoprefixer strategy?
advanced.answer: Vendor prefixes (-webkit-, -moz-, -ms-, -o-) are treated as distinct property names by the cascade. If multiple prefixed versions and the standard version have equal specificity, the last one in source order wins. The best practice is always to place the unprefixed standard property last. Autoprefixer automatically parses CSS and adds/removes vendor prefixes based on Can I Use data, ensuring only needed prefixes are included.

advanced.question: Explain the scenario where an element has both inline styles and !important from a stylesheet — which wins?
advanced.answer: Both inline styles and !important affect the cascade. When comparing, the browser considers both origin and specificity. An !important declaration in an external stylesheet will override a normal (non-important) inline style because !important changes the origin priority. However, an !important inline style (style="color: red !important;") will override an !important rule from a stylesheet because inline styles have a higher specificity origin tier.

advanced.question: How does the CSS cascade interact with CSS Custom Properties (variables)?
advanced.answer: CSS Custom Properties are resolved after the cascade has been applied. The cascade determines which custom property value is applied to an element, then the var() references are resolved using the computed value. This means custom properties inherit through the DOM and can be overridden by specificity, source order, and !important just like regular properties. However, invalid var() references can cause the property to inherit or use its initial value.

advanced.question: What is the difference between the rendered font size in CSS pixels versus device pixels, and how does this relate to the cascade?
advanced.answer: The cascade determines the computed value of font-size in CSS pixels, but the actual rendered size depends on device pixel ratio, browser zoom level, and user font-size settings. For example, a 16px computed value might render as 32 device pixels on a 2x Retina display, or 20 CSS pixels at 125% zoom. The cascade operates on the author-defined values, but the visual output depends on these rendering factors that happen after cascade resolution.

advanced.question: How do you implement a CSS architecture that prevents cascade conflicts in a large team project?
advanced.answer: Strategies include: (1) Using a naming methodology like BEM to create unique, predictable class names; (2) Using CSS Modules or scoped styles to encapsulate component styles; (3) Adopting Cascade Layers (@layer) to explicitly control layer priority; (4) Setting a global CSS reset to eliminate cross-browser differences; (5) Using CSS Custom Properties for theming instead of overriding; (6) Establishing a z-index scale system via custom properties; (7) Implementing code review processes that enforce low-specificity selectors.
